planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–April or September–November. Fireworks Goldenrod spreads steadily by underground rhizomes — divide every 2–3 years when clumps become crowded. Unlike taller goldenrod species, Fireworks remains compact and does not flop without support. The arching, fountain-like flowering stems reach 3–4 feet in the August–October bloom season — timing that fills the critical late-summer to fall gap in the native perennial garden. Goldenrod does NOT cause hay fever — the airborne pollen responsible for fall allergies comes from ragweed (Ambrosia) which blooms simultaneously. Goldenrod's heavy, sticky pollen is designed for bee transport, not wind dispersal.

directions: Plant in full sun to light partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Fireworks Goldenrod adapts to a wide range of soil conditions and is drought tolerant once established; Fireworks is a cultivar of Wrinkle-Leaf Goldenrod (Solidago rugosa), selected for its distinctive, highly ornamental, arching flower plumes — the yellow flower branches radiate outward and downward from the main stem in a pattern that genuinely resembles an exploding firework, creating one of the most architecturally interesting flower forms available among native fall perennials. Unlike the more common Canada Goldenrod (S. canadensis) with its straight, upright flower plumes, Fireworks' arching, fountain-like sprays create a different kind of visual movement in the fall garden — the gentle arching heavy with tiny yellow flowers creating a horizontal spray pattern that photographs beautifully and provides an interesting contrast to the upright forms of fall asters and ironweeds. Goldenrod is arguably the most important late-season pollinator plant in the Eastern US — supporting more than 100 bee species (including specialist bees that only use goldenrod) as both a nectar and pollen source during September and October when insects are building winter energy reserves. As a native species throughout the Gulf South, Solidago rugosa 'Fireworks' supports the local beneficial insect community with zero cultivation investment after establishment.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Solidago rugosa Native Plant Profile
North Carolina State University Extension — Solidago rugosa 'Fireworks' Plant Profile
University of Georgia Extension — Native Goldenrod for Southern Gardens

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–April or September–November. Vivid Obedient Plant spreads by underground rhizomes — divide every 2–3 years to prevent aggressive spreading in smaller gardens. The common name 'Obedient Plant' comes from the unusual behavior of the individual flowers: each bloom can be pushed to one side on the flower spike and will stay in its new position ('obediently'), making it interesting for demonstration and a unique cut flower. In zone 9 the late-summer bloom (August–October) aligns with hummingbird migration, making the tubular pink flowers an important nectar source for migrating Ruby-throated Hummingbirds.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Vivid Obedient Plant performs well in partial shade and tolerates the moist, partially shaded conditions along stream banks and pond margins where it naturally grows; Vivid is a compact cultivar of Obedient Plant (Physostegia virginiana), a native eastern North American perennial producing upright, four-angled stems topped with dense, terminal spikes of tubular rose-pink to magenta flowers in late summer and fall. The 'Vivid' selection was chosen for its particularly rich, vivid rose-pink color and compact habit (24–30 inches rather than the 3–5 foot height of the unimproved species), making it much more manageable in garden settings where the species' tendency to flop and spread aggressively is less welcome. The late-season bloom timing of Obedient Plant (August–October in zone 9) is one of its most valuable landscape qualities — providing strong pink color in the native perennial garden at exactly the moment when most spring and summer native perennials have finished blooming and fall asters haven't yet reached peak. As a native species, Physostegia supports native bumblebees, long-tongued bees, and hummingbirds with its abundant nectar.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Physostegia virginiana Native Plant
North Carolina State University Extension — Obedient Plant for Southern Gardens
University of Georgia Extension — Native Perennials for Fall Interest

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–April or September–November. Siskiyou Pink Gaura is a Texas native (Oenothera lindheimeri, formerly Gaura lindheimeri) that is exceptionally well-adapted to the dry, hot conditions of zone 9 and thrives in the lean, well-drained soils of the Gulf Coast uplands. Provide excellent drainage — gaura is susceptible to root rot in wet or poorly drained soils. In zone 9 it blooms from early spring through frost in an essentially uninterrupted display of the dancing, butterfly-like pink flowers on wiry stems. Cut back by one-third in midsummer if the plant becomes too open and floppy, which stimulates fresh, compact growth.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Siskiyou Pink Gaura demands excellent drainage and thrives in dry, sunny conditions but declines in consistently moist or heavy soil; Siskiyou Pink is a cultivar of Lindheimer's Gaura (Oenothera lindheimeri, formerly classified as Gaura lindheimeri), a native Texas wildflower producing a cloud of small, four-petaled, pink to rose-pink flowers on slender, wiry, arching stems that wave and dance in the slightest breeze — creating a distinctive, airy, naturalistic movement in the garden that earned the plant the descriptive nickname 'whirling butterflies.' Siskiyou Pink is specifically selected for deeper, more saturated pink flower color than the predominantly white or pale-pink straight species, making the floral display more vivid and garden-worthy while retaining the characteristic light, dancing quality of the stem-and-flower form. The combination of essentially continuous bloom from spring through frost, extreme drought tolerance, heat tolerance in zones 6–9, and the light, airy, naturalistic flower form that works in both formal and cottage garden styles has made Gaura one of the most universally successful flowering perennials for the modern Southern garden. Its native Texas origin means full adaptation to the hot, dry conditions of zone 9 summers.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Oenothera lindheimeri Native Plant
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Gaura lindheimeri for Texas Landscapes
University of Georgia Extension — Drought-Tolerant Perennials for Southern Gardens

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–April or September–November. Little Goldstar is an All-America Selections winner selected for compact form (16–20 inches vs 24–30 for 'Goldsturm') and extended bloom season. The mounding, non-flopping habit holds its form through bloom without staking — a consistent problem with standard-sized Rudbeckia cultivars in humid, fertile conditions. Deadhead or cut back spent flower stems to stimulate additional flowering; the main bloom is August–October in zone 9. Divide every 3–4 years when clumps become crowded and flower production declines.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Little Goldstar Rudbeckia adapts to a wide range of soils, is drought tolerant once established, and blooms most freely in full sun; Little Goldstar is an All-America Selections award-winning compact cultivar of Orange Coneflower (Rudbeckia fulgida), producing a dense, mounding form covered with the classic single, golden-yellow daisy flowers with prominent dark brown central cones that define the black-eyed susan. The compact selection (16–20 inches) is specifically bred to address the most common problem with standard Rudbeckia — the tendency to flop and decline in the heat and humidity of Southern gardens — by reducing height, increasing stem stiffness, and selecting for heat tolerance. Black-eyed susans are the quintessential late-summer and fall native perennial for the American garden, providing the most vivid and extensive golden-yellow display available from any native plant in the August–October timeframe when garden color is often difficult to maintain. As a native plant across the Eastern US, Rudbeckia fulgida supports native bees, seed-eating birds (goldfinches prize the seed heads), and beneficial insects throughout the blooming and seeding period that extends from summer through winter.
sources: All-America Selections — Rudbeckia Little Goldstar Award
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Rudbeckia fulgida Native Plant
North Carolina State University Extension — Compact Rudbeckia Cultivars

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April or September–November. Cheyenne Spirit is unusual among Echinacea cultivars in producing true-to-color seed — seeds collected from Cheyenne Spirit plants will produce seedlings with the same range of colors as the parent generation, making seed saving practical. In zone 9 coneflowers are at their best in spring (March–May) and fall (September–October), often going semi-dormant in the peak of summer heat. Provide excellent drainage — Echinacea tolerates drought but is susceptible to root rot in consistently wet soil. Allow some seed heads to mature for winter bird interest (goldfinches especially prize Echinacea seeds).

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Cheyenne Spirit Echinacea demands excellent drainage and thrives in the dry, sunny conditions that replicate its native prairie habitat; Cheyenne Spirit is an All-America Selections award-winning Echinacea hybrid that was groundbreaking at its release for producing a true mixed-color coneflower that could be grown from seed — an unusual achievement among modern Echinacea hybrids, most of which are sterile or produce off-color seedlings. The seed-grown mix produces coneflowers in an unexpectedly wide range of colors including red, orange, yellow, cream, white, and purple — colors across almost the entire warm and cool spectrum — creating a naturalistic wildflower planting of varied, harmonious color from a single seed packet. Each individual plant produces flowers in one consistent color throughout its life, making the mixed effect something that develops across the planting rather than within individual plants. The combination of genuine native-area adaptation (Echinacea purpurea is native across much of the Eastern and Central US), exceptional bee and butterfly value, drought tolerance, and the genuine mixed-color seed innovation that allows informal, naturalistic planting without purchasing multiple named cultivars makes Cheyenne Spirit one of the most useful single Echinacea varieties for zone 9 pollinator gardens.
sources: All-America Selections — Echinacea Cheyenne Spirit Award
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Echinacea purpurea Native Plant
University of Georgia Extension — Coneflower Varieties for Southern Gardens
